milestone_description: Transform RASS from an engineering demonstration into a compelling, interview-ready knowledge discovery platform. This phase focuses on demo UX quality, comprehensive documentation, one-click setup, and the narrative that makes RASS stand out as a portfolio piece and enterprise showcase.

issues.title: [Phase F] Redesign demo UX with knowledge graph visualization and conversational context
issues.body:
## Problem

The current React frontend is functional but minimal. For a showcase or demo setting, it lacks:
- Visual differentiation from generic chat UIs
- Knowledge graph visualization showing how documents relate to each other
- Conversation context panel showing which parts of which documents informed each answer
- An impressive, polished first impression for technical interviewers or enterprise evaluators

A great demo UI is the difference between a project that gets noticed and one that gets forgotten.

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Knowledge graph visualization panel
- Add `react-force-graph-2d` (WebGL-based, performant) to the frontend
- After ingestion, compute a document similarity graph by comparing chunk embeddings (cosine similarity)
- Expose `GET /api/knowledge-bases/:kbId/graph` that returns `{ nodes: [{id, label, chunkCount}], edges: [{source, target, weight}] }`
- Render an interactive force-directed graph in a sidebar panel
- Clicking a node filters the document library and highlights related documents

### 2. Source attribution timeline
- Below each answer, render a horizontal timeline showing which documents contributed to the answer
- Each segment is proportional to the number of cited chunks from that document
- Hovering a segment expands the relevant excerpt

### 3. Conversational context panel
- Add a collapsible right sidebar: "What RASS is thinking"
- Shows: the reformulated query (HyDE or planner output), the top-5 retrieved chunks with relevance scores, and the rerank order change
- Makes the retrieval pipeline transparent and impressive in demos

### 4. Demo mode
- Add `DEMO_MODE: true` in config; in demo mode:
  - Pre-load a curated set of documents on first run
  - Show a banner: "Try asking: [example queries]"
  - Disable document deletion

### 5. Visual design polish
- Consistent color palette, typography, and spacing (use Material-UI theme tokens)
- Animated streaming text with a blinking cursor during generation
- Smooth transitions between chat, document library, and graph views

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] The knowledge graph panel renders correctly with a corpus of ≥ 5 documents
- [ ] Clicking a graph node filters the document library to related documents
- [ ] The "RASS is thinking" sidebar shows retrieved chunks and rerank scores for each query
- [ ] Demo mode pre-loads sample documents and shows example queries on first run
- [ ] The UI is visually consistent (font, spacing, color) across all panels
- [ ] Animated streaming text renders without flickering or layout shifts
- [ ] The UI is usable on a 1080p screen without horizontal scrolling

issues.title: [Phase F] Create comprehensive API documentation with OpenAPI 3.1 spec
issues.body:
## Problem

RAGS has no API documentation. Developers wanting to integrate with RASS, build custom frontends, or use the MCP tools must read the source code to discover endpoints, request/response shapes, and authentication requirements. This is a significant barrier to adoption and integration.

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Write the OpenAPI 3.1 spec
Create `docs/api/openapi.yaml` covering all mcp-server endpoints:
- Auth: `POST /api/auth/register`, `POST /api/auth/login`, `POST /api/auth/refresh`
- Documents: `GET /api/documents`, `GET /api/documents/:id`, `DELETE /api/documents/:id`, `GET /api/documents/:id/provenance`
- Ingestion: `POST /api/embed-upload`, `GET /ingest/status/:jobId`
- Search: `POST /api/stream-ask` (SSE stream, documented with response event types)
- Knowledge Bases: CRUD endpoints from Phase B/D
- Workspaces: CRUD endpoints from Phase D
- Admin: `GET /api/admin/audit-logs`, `GET /api/admin/audit-logs/export`

Use `$ref` for reusable schemas: `Citation`, `Document`, `KnowledgeBase`, `Workspace`, `User`.

### 2. Serve interactive docs via Swagger UI
- Add `swagger-ui-express` to mcp-server
- Mount at `/api/docs` (disabled in production via config)
- Auto-validate request/response schemas against the spec using `express-openapi-validator`

### 3. Generate a typed API client
- Add a `npm run generate:client` script that runs `openapi-typescript-codegen` to generate a TypeScript client in `frontend/src/api/generated/`
- Replace hand-written `axios` calls in the frontend with the generated client

### 4. Document the SSE streaming protocol
- Write `docs/api/streaming.md` describing the SSE event types, their schemas, and the client-side handling pattern

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `docs/api/openapi.yaml` is valid OpenAPI 3.1 (validated by `vacuum lint` or `spectral lint`)
- [ ] All mcp-server endpoints are documented with request/response schemas and example values
- [ ] Swagger UI is accessible at `http://localhost:8080/api/docs` in the development stack
- [ ] `npm run generate:client` produces a valid TypeScript client from the spec
- [ ] `express-openapi-validator` middleware rejects requests that do not match the spec
- [ ] `docs/api/streaming.md` accurately describes all SSE event types with examples

issues.title: [Phase F] Create a one-click demo setup with pre-seeded data and guided tour
issues.body:
## Problem

Setting up RASS for a demo or evaluation requires:
1. Copying `.env` files
2. Adjusting `config.yml`
3. Running `docker compose up -d`
4. Waiting for services to be healthy
5. Registering a user account
6. Manually uploading sample documents
7. Running the first query

This 7-step process is a friction point that discourages evaluators and makes live demos risky. A first-time experience that "just works" is essential for showcasing the system.

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Create a `demo/` setup
- `demo/docker-compose.demo.yml` — overrides the main compose file with demo-specific settings (demo credentials, pre-seeded data, no auth required for read operations)
- `demo/seed_data/` — a curated set of 5–10 sample documents (medical informatics, research papers, technical documentation) in PDF and DOCX format
- `demo/seed.sh` — seeds the demo user account, ingests all sample documents, and waits for ingestion to complete

### 2. One-command startup
```bash
# scripts/demo.sh
cp demo/.env.demo .env
docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f demo/docker-compose.demo.yml up -d --build
bash demo/seed.sh
echo "✅ RASS Demo ready at http://localhost:8080"
echo "   Login: demo@rass.ai / demo-password"
```

### 3. In-app guided tour
- Add a `react-joyride` guided tour that activates on first login:
  1. "Here are your documents" → document library
  2. "Try asking a question" → chat input
  3. "See where the answer came from" → citations panel
  4. "Explore the knowledge graph" → graph panel
- Tour can be dismissed and relaunched from the Help menu

### 4. Example query chips
- In the chat input area, show 3 clickable example queries when the chat is empty
- Queries are loaded from `demo/example_queries.json` and match the seeded documents

### 5. Health check dashboard
- Add `GET /api/health` that returns service health for all dependencies (OpenSearch, Redis, Postgres, BullMQ queue)
- Show a health indicator badge in the frontend corner

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Running `bash scripts/demo.sh` from a clean checkout produces a working RASS instance in under 5 minutes
- [ ] The demo user is auto-created and pre-authenticated in the browser on first visit
- [ ] All sample documents are ingested and searchable within 2 minutes of `demo.sh` completion
- [ ] The guided tour launches automatically on first login and covers all four steps
- [ ] Example query chips populate the correct answers from the seeded documents
- [ ] `GET /api/health` returns `200 OK` with status for all dependencies
- [ ] The setup works on macOS, Linux, and Windows (WSL2)

issues.title: [Phase F] Write comprehensive developer and architecture documentation
issues.body:
## Problem

While `README.md` and `DEPLOYMENT.md` exist, the documentation is insufficient for:
- A new developer to understand and contribute to the codebase
- An interviewer or technical evaluator to understand the architecture decisions
- An operator to deploy RASS in production
- A user to understand how to get the most out of the system

Good documentation is a force multiplier and a key quality signal in interview portfolios.

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Architecture Decision Records (ADRs)
Create `docs/adr/` with records for key design decisions:
- `001-multi-service-architecture.md` — why microservices vs. monolith
- `002-opensearch-vector-db.md` — why OpenSearch vs. Pinecone/Weaviate/pgvector
- `003-parent-child-chunking.md` — why hierarchical chunking strategy
- `004-hybrid-retrieval.md` — why KNN + BM25 vs. vector-only
- `005-bullmq-job-queue.md` — why BullMQ vs. SQS/Celery
- `006-sse-streaming.md` — why SSE vs. WebSocket

Each ADR follows the standard format: Context, Decision, Consequences.

### 2. Enhanced README
- Add a system architecture diagram (Mermaid) showing all services and data flows
- Add a "How retrieval works" section with a step-by-step numbered list
- Add a "Configuration reference" table for all `config.yml` keys
- Add a "Troubleshooting" section for the 5 most common issues

### 3. Contributing guide
Create `CONTRIBUTING.md`:
- Development environment setup (with and without Docker)
- Code style guide (ESLint config, naming conventions)
- How to add a new chunking strategy
- How to add a new retrieval stage
- How to add test cases to the evaluation dataset
- PR checklist

### 4. Deployment guide
Expand `DEPLOYMENT.md`:
- Cloud deployment guides: AWS ECS, GCP Cloud Run, Azure Container Apps
- Kubernetes Helm chart outline
- Environment variable reference
- Production hardening checklist (TLS, secrets management, backup)

### 5. User guide
Create `docs/user-guide.md`:
- How to upload and manage documents
- How to create and manage knowledge bases
- How to interpret citations and relevance scores
- How to use the knowledge graph view

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Six ADRs are created and clearly explain the reasoning behind each architectural decision
- [ ] README includes a Mermaid architecture diagram, configuration reference table, and troubleshooting section
- [ ] `CONTRIBUTING.md` explains how to add a new retrieval stage with a worked example
- [ ] `DEPLOYMENT.md` includes step-by-step guides for at least two cloud platforms
- [ ] All documentation is technically accurate (verified by following each guide on a clean machine)
- [ ] The Mermaid diagram renders correctly in GitHub's markdown renderer

issues.title: [Phase F] Build a showcase value narrative with recorded demo and case study
issues.body:
## Problem

RASS has strong technical fundamentals but no story. Without a clear narrative explaining:
- What problem it solves
- For whom
- What makes it different from a generic RAG tutorial
- What real-world impact it could have

...the system is technically impressive but professionally invisible.

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Define the value proposition
Create `docs/VALUE_PROPOSITION.md`:
- Target users: knowledge workers, medical informatics teams, legal/compliance teams, research teams
- Problem: information is siloed in documents; finding the right information requires reading dozens of files manually
- Solution: RASS makes any document corpus queryable in natural language, with citations that let you verify every answer
- Differentiators: enterprise-grade isolation, HyDE query expansion, cross-encoder reranking, structured citations, live observability

### 2. Record a 3-minute demo video
- Script covering: upload a document → ask a question → get a cited answer → view the knowledge graph
- Record at 1080p with narration explaining what is happening and why it matters
- Upload to YouTube (unlisted) and embed in `README.md`

### 3. Write a case study
Create `docs/CASE_STUDY.md`:
- Scenario: a medical informatics team at a 50-person hospital needs to query clinical guidelines and research papers
- Before RASS: 45 minutes to find and synthesize information from 3 PDFs
- After RASS: 30-second query with cited answer and confidence scores
- Metrics: estimated time savings, accuracy comparison, user satisfaction

### 4. Performance summary card
Create `docs/PERFORMANCE.md`:
- Key metrics: query latency p95, ingestion throughput, concurrent user capacity
- Retrieval quality: TruLens scores (faithfulness, relevance, context recall)
- Comparison to RAG baseline (no reranking, no HyDE)

### 5. LinkedIn / portfolio write-up
- Draft a 500-word technical blog post in `docs/BLOG_POST.md` describing the system, the engineering decisions, and lessons learned

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `docs/VALUE_PROPOSITION.md` clearly articulates the problem, solution, and differentiators in non-technical language
- [ ] A demo video script is written and reviewed for clarity and impact
- [ ] `docs/CASE_STUDY.md` presents a realistic before/after scenario with estimated metrics
- [ ] `docs/PERFORMANCE.md` contains real (measured) latency and quality numbers from Phase E benchmarking
- [ ] `docs/BLOG_POST.md` is a complete, technically accurate, and engaging 500-word post
- [ ] All documentation links from `README.md` resolve correctly
